promptExample.label: Support Ticket Urgency Classifier
promptExample.prompt:
System: You are a support ticket triage assistant.
Classify each ticket by urgency: critical, high, medium, or low.
Respond with only the urgency label.

---
Ticket: "My account was charged twice this month. Need a refund ASAP."
Urgency: critical

Ticket: "Can I upgrade my plan mid-cycle?"
Urgency: low

Ticket: "The app crashes every time I try to export a report."
Urgency: high
---

User: Ticket: "Would be nice if you added dark mode someday."
Urgency:

eli3: Few-shot prompting provides a small number of (input, output) examples in the prompt before the actual query, showing the model exactly what format and behavior you expect. 'Example: Input: The movie was terrible. Output: negative. Input: Loved it! Output: positive. Input: [new review]. Output:' — the model continues the pattern. No fine-tuning, no gradient updates — just examples in context.

GPT-3 (Brown et al. 2020) demonstrated that large LMs are powerful few-shot learners: performance scales with the number of examples (1-shot, 5-shot, 10-shot) up to a point. Why it works: the model's in-context attention treats the examples as local 'training data' — it infers the task format, label space, and mapping pattern. This is distinct from fine-tuning (no weights are updated).

Few-shot prompting is most effective when: examples are high-quality and representative, labels are balanced, and the task format is consistent across examples and the test query. Example order matters: recent examples (near the query) have more influence than early ones (primacy/recency effects). Few-shot can be outperformed by zero-shot + CoT for reasoning tasks, but is generally stronger for structured output and format-sensitive tasks.

whenWhy.title: Reliable format control for structured outputs
whenWhy.body: When the output must follow a specific format (JSON, table, code, specific label scheme), few-shot examples demonstrate the exact structure. Example extraction of structured data from text: show 2–3 examples of (text, JSON output) pairs, then pass the new text. The model reliably imitates the format shown. This is more robust than just describing the format in zero-shot because the model sees exact instantiations. Particularly useful for data extraction, form filling, and entity normalization tasks.

whenWhy.title: Adapting to custom label sets and taxonomies
whenWhy.body: Fine-tuning a model for a custom 15-class taxonomy requires labeled data and training time. Few-shot prompting with one example per class provides instant adaptation. The model learns the label names and mapping from just the examples. For classification with unusual or domain-specific labels ('escalation risk: low/medium/high/critical'), few-shot examples communicate semantics that instruction text alone cannot fully convey. This is particularly useful for prototyping before committing to fine-tuning.

whenWhy.title: Improving accuracy on harder tasks
whenWhy.body: For tasks where zero-shot fails (structured NLI, complex slot-filling, domain-specific annotation), few-shot examples significantly improve accuracy. Brown et al. (2020): GPT-3 SuperGLUE zero-shot: 71.8%, 32-shot: 73.1% (fine-tuned BERT-large: 89%). On closed-book QA (TriviaQA), 64-shot GPT-3: 71.2% vs zero-shot: 64.3%. More examples help but with diminishing returns; beyond 10–20 examples, gains are minimal and cost increases linearly.

deepDiveIntro: In-context learning (ICL) mechanics: when provided with k examples (xᵢ, yᵢ), the model conditions P(y|x₁,y₁,…,xₖ,yₖ,x) on the entire demonstration sequence. Hypothesis (Xie et al. 2022): ICL is implicit Bayesian inference — the model implicitly infers the task from the demonstrations. The attention mechanism allows the model to 'look up' similar examples and apply the demonstrated mapping.

Prompt construction best practices: (1) Label balance — include examples from all classes; (2) Format consistency — same format across all examples and query; (3) Diversity — diverse inputs prevent the model from memorizing surface patterns; (4) Quality — incorrect examples hurt performance (learning the wrong mapping). Template: '[example 1]
[example 2]
…
[query]
'. Delimiter choice: '###', '---', XML tags all work. Separate demonstrations from query clearly.

deepDive.heading: What Few-Shot ICL Learns from Demonstrations
deepDive.body: Min et al. (2022) — 'Rethinking Role of Demonstrations': in few-shot ICL, the model learns (1) the output format/space (what types of outputs are expected), and (2) the input distribution — but NOT necessarily the input-label mapping. Experiment: replacing true labels with random labels in demonstrations barely hurts accuracy on classification tasks (−5–10%). Implication: demonstrations mostly teach format and output space, not the label meaning. The mapping is already in the pre-trained weights. This explains why ICL works with few examples but also why it can fail on tasks that require new knowledge not in pre-training. [1]

deepDive.heading: Example Selection and Ordering
deepDive.body: Example selection strategies: (1) Random sampling from a pool (simple baseline); (2) Similarity-based retrieval — find the k training examples most similar to the test query (kNN using embedding similarity); (3) Diversity-based — maximize diversity to cover the input space. Ordering effects: recency bias — examples closer to the query have more influence. For balanced classification, put examples of each class near the end. Label balance: unbalanced demonstrations bias predictions toward majority class. Calibration (Zhao et al. 2021): use a bias-correction step to account for prior label imbalance in demonstrations. [2]

deepDive.heading: Many-Shot ICL with Long Context Windows
deepDive.body: Agarwal et al. (2024) — 'Many-Shot In-Context Learning': with long context windows (128K+ tokens), provide hundreds to thousands of examples. Many-shot vs few-shot: on medical diagnosis, 2048 examples vs 64 examples improves accuracy from 60% to 90%. Many-shot can override model priors — teach the model new mappings not in pre-training. But requires a large labeled pool, and cost scales linearly with example count. Example selection becomes critical at many-shot scale — random selection underperforms retrieval-augmented selection. [2]

deepDive.heading: Few-Shot CoT and Self-Consistency
deepDive.body: Few-shot CoT (Wei et al. 2022): provide examples with step-by-step reasoning traces as demonstrations. 'Q: John has 5 apples... A: John has 5 apples. He gives 2 away. 5-2=3. So John has 3 apples.' The model imitates the reasoning pattern. Self-consistency (Wang et al. 2023): sample multiple (k=40) reasoning paths for the same query, take majority vote answer. On GSM8K math: few-shot CoT alone 56.5%, + self-consistency 78.0% (GPT-3 175B). Self-consistency is the most reliable few-shot prompting strategy for reasoning tasks — marginalizes over different reasoning paths. [3]
deepDive.formula: Self-consistency: ŷ = argmax_y Σₖ 𝟙[f(xᵢ, demoₖ) = y]

takeaways: Few-shot: k labeled examples in context; teaches format, output space, and pattern by demonstration. [1]
Min et al. (2022): demonstrations teach output format/space, not necessarily input-label mapping. [1]
Recency bias: later examples have more influence — put balanced examples near the query. [2]
Many-shot (hundreds of examples): overrides model priors, works best with retrieval-based selection. [2]
Self-consistency: majority vote over k=40 reasoning paths — best few-shot reasoning strategy. [3]

edgeCases.q: How many few-shot examples are typically optimal?
edgeCases.a: Empirically: k=4–8 examples is the sweet spot for most tasks. Gains diminish beyond k=10–20 examples. GPT-3 paper: 1-shot, 10-shot, 100-shot — incremental gains, but 100-shot is rarely 2× better than 10-shot. Many-shot (Agarwal 2024) shows large gains at 2048 examples but only with 128K+ context windows. Optimal k depends on: task complexity (harder tasks benefit from more examples), label space size (more classes → more examples needed), and model size (larger models benefit more from additional examples). Budget constraint: each example costs input tokens — balance accuracy gain vs. API cost. In practice, evaluate k=0,1,3,5,8 and pick the best for your quality/cost threshold. [1][2]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between few-shot prompting and fine-tuning?
edgeCases.a: Few-shot prompting: examples are in the context (input tokens), no weight updates. Inference time only. Benefits: no training cost, instantly switchable, works with API models. Drawbacks: examples consume context, cost per inference scales with example count, can't learn truly new knowledge. Fine-tuning: gradient updates adjust model weights on labeled examples. Benefits: persistent learning (not in context), cheaper inference (no example tokens), can learn new mappings and domain knowledge. Drawbacks: training cost, needs more examples (>100 for LoRA, >1K for full fine-tune), requires model access. Rule of thumb: few-shot if <100 examples or API-only; fine-tune if >500 examples and accuracy matters significantly. [1]

sources.label: Brown et al. (2020) — Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3), NeurIPS 2020
Min et al. (2022) — Rethinking the Role of Demonstrations, EMNLP 2022
Wang et al. (2023) — Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models, ICLR 2023
